System clocks capture times without manual transcription lag. Automatic timestamping reduces operator burden but manual back-dating still requires oversight. Timestamps diverging from actual execution cause costly integrity questions.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that retained batch data cannot be retrieved in complete and readable form for assessment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that stored batch information cannot be found or read in full when it needs to be reviewed. Complete, readable records help people make sound decisions and show what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Retained data not retrievable in complete, readable form defeats retention purpose; central to lifecycle integrity and severe for inspection and investigation needs.
rows.sat_rat: Records are usually retrievable and readable within retention systems, but long-term format obsolescence and migration remain real risks. Retrieval of retained records is quick within the active system window. Standard retrieval needs little skill, though long-term archive access can require specialists. Archiving and migration to preserve readability carry ongoing storage and validation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required batch evidence missing when compliance review begins
rows.plain: Have nearly all required batch records ready when the compliance check starts. This makes the review faster and avoids delays.
rows.imp_rat: Missing required evidence when compliance review begins impairs the assessment; central to readiness and severe if review proceeds regardless.
rows.sat_rat: EBR completeness checks catch many gaps but missing required evidence at review start remains a common recurring occurrence. Chasing missing evidence at review start delays disposition while records are collected. Knowing exactly which evidence is required before review begins depends on experienced reviewers rather than the system enforcing it. Rework and delayed batch release consume resources when evidence turns up missing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accessing a superseded work instruction for the active sterile fill-finish step
rows.plain: Make sure workers use the current work instructions for the active step of filling and finishing the product in a germ-free environment. This helps prevent outdated directions from causing mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Accessing a superseded work instruction for an active sterile step defeats the MES's job of enforcing correct procedures (high relevance) and is severe because it can produce out-of-spec or non-compliant sterile batches.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced document version control tied to the active step is a core, mature MES strength that rarely fails when deployed. MES serves the current effective work instruction instantly at the step, eliminating time spent locating the right revision. Version control removes the operator's burden of judging which instruction is current. Controlled document delivery avoids rework from wrong-revision execution at modest system cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed batch steps lacking required operator or reviewer sign-offs
rows.plain: Make sure every completed batch step has the required worker and reviewer signatures. This helps show that the work was done and checked properly.
rows.imp_rat: Completed steps lacking required operator/reviewer sign-offs leave work uncertified; central to complete capture and severe when unsigned steps reach disposition.
rows.sat_rat: Sign-off enforcement at step completion is a well-established EBR control, making unsigned completed steps uncommon. Workflow gating prevents step closure without required sign-offs, avoiding later rework. Enforced sign-off steps reduce dependence on reviewer vigilance. Missing sign-offs caught in-line avoid costly disposition delays.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that displayed throughput, yield, or OEE values misrepresent actual sterile fill-finish results
rows.plain: Make sure the displayed production numbers accurately show how much was made, how much met requirements, and how efficiently the work ran.
rows.imp_rat: Misrepresented throughput/yield/OEE for sterile fill-finish directly corrupts the measure-and-control CFJ (high relevance), and wrong metrics can drive bad production decisions or mask quality issues (high severity), though these are derived KPIs rather than raw source data. Capped at 7.5 mean per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Metric accuracy depends on correct source-tag mapping and calculation definitions, which commonly harbor silent errors, leaving this only acceptably served. Dashboards refresh throughput/yield/OEE in near real time, so latency is rarely the issue. Displayed metrics reduce the need for manual calculation but interpreting anomalies still requires analyst judgement. Misrepresented metrics drive poor decisions with real cost, and validating the calculation logic takes effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required execution fields blank at sterile fill-finish step completion
rows.plain: Fewer required entries should be left blank when the sterile filling and finishing step is complete. Recording the information at the time of work makes the production record more complete and reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Blank required fields at sterile step completion is a completeness gap with elevated severity for sterile products, but typically caught before release.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory-field enforcement in EBR helps where present, but hybrid/paper environments still leave blanks. Checking for blank fields at step completion is a brief added task. Detecting blanks relies on operator and verifier attention rather than automated blocking on paper. Blanks found later require costly retrospective completion and justification.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of corrections lacking a traceable link to the original entry
rows.plain: Make every correction in a record clearly traceable to the original entry. This makes it possible to see what was changed and keeps the record trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Corrections without a traceable link to the original entry break the audit trail; central to integrity and severe for defensibility.
rows.sat_rat: GDP correction rules are mature and widely enforced, so traceability is fairly reliable. Adding a traceable link (initials, date, reason) is a quick standard act. Maintaining traceability depends on operators following GDP conventions. Untraceable corrections trigger data-integrity findings that are costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of execution entries lacking required performer or verifier signatures
rows.plain: Ensure that nearly all work records include the required signatures from the person who did the work and the person who checked it. This shows who performed and verified each step.
rows.imp_rat: Execution entries lacking required performer/verifier signatures break attributability and independent verification; central to ALCOA capture and severe for accountability.
rows.sat_rat: Performer/verifier e-signature enforcement is mature, making entries without required signatures rare where configured. E-signature prompts enforce signatures at the point of entry without later chasing. Enforced signature workflows remove reliance on reviewers to detect missing signatures. Complete signatures avoid costly review returns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that entries from different batches, lots, containers, equipment, or personnel are reconciled together
rows.plain: Make sure information from different batches, lots, containers, equipment, or workers is not combined by mistake. This keeps records connected to the correct materials and people.
rows.imp_rat: Reconciling entries across different batches/lots/equipment cross-contaminates the record structure, undermining the job and risking wrong-batch release decisions.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced identity binding largely prevents cross-batch reconciliation, though shared equipment and manual entries leave residual failure modes. System-enforced context tags let the record assemble without manual cross-checking of each entry's origin. Barcode/equipment-ID scanning reduces reliance on operator vigilance to keep lots and containers separate. Prevented cross-contamination-of-data errors avoid expensive batch investigations, but edge cases still incur cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the approved instruction version governing an execution entry cannot be established
rows.plain: Make sure it is possible to determine which approved version of the instructions was used for each recorded step. This shows whether the work followed the correct instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to establish the approved instruction version governing an entry undermines what the data means; central to interpretation and severe when it invalidates the review basis.
rows.sat_rat: EBRs generally record the executed MBR version reliably, though referenced ancillary procedures are less consistently version-bound. Recorded MBR version links let reviewers establish the governing version quickly. Version stamping reduces the expertise needed to trace which approved instruction applied. Establishable versions avoid costly re-review and investigation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of evidence items attributed to the wrong batch, operation, intervention, or timestamp
rows.plain: Reduce the number of records or other proof linked to the wrong batch, activity, worker action, or time. Correct links help show what really happened.
rows.imp_rat: Evidence attributed to the wrong batch/operation/intervention/timestamp corrupts traceability; central to accurate structure and severe when it misinforms disposition.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic capture reliably attributes most events, but interventions logged out-of-band can still be misattributed. Timestamp and operation linking is automatic, saving manual attribution time. Automated capture reduces dependence on operator care to attribute events correctly. Fewer misattributions reduce investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of disposition evidence items associated with the wrong batch, material lot, equipment, or processing step
rows.plain: Make sure every piece of evidence used to decide whether a batch can be released belongs to the correct batch, material, equipment, and processing step. This prevents the wrong information from supporting a batch decision.
rows.imp_rat: Disposition evidence tied to the wrong batch/lot/equipment/step misdirects the release decision; central to structural accuracy and severe when it releases a different batch.
rows.sat_rat: EBR context binding makes misattributed evidence fairly infrequent, though manual attachments remain a weak point. Automated batch/lot/equipment linking speeds correct association versus manual tagging. System-enforced context reduces reliance on operators correctly attributing each item. Misattribution rework is reduced by barcode/system linkage, lowering cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of captured machine values differing from source readings during sterile fill-finish production
rows.plain: The values recorded from the machine should match the values actually shown by the machine. This helps ensure that production records are accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Captured machine values differing from source readings undermines the measurement foundation of the MES (high relevance) and in sterile fill-finish can lead to undetected out-of-spec batches (high severity). Held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Captured-vs-source fidelity is good on integrated equipment but drift, buffering, and unit-scaling mismatches still cause recurring discrepancies. Direct machine integration captures values in real time, eliminating transcription lag. Automated data capture removes the operator skill needed for manual readings. Interface engineering carries cost but avoids the expense of manual data reconciliation.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch disposition rules lacking validation criteria for status transitions, approvals, and release restrictions
rows.plain: Create clear checks for deciding whether a production batch changes status, who must approve it, and when it must not be released. This helps ensure every batch decision is properly supported.
rows.imp_rat: Disposition rules lacking validation criteria for status transitions and release restrictions can allow improper releases; moderately central to review controls and severe for release integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Configurable workflow states help, but validation criteria for every transition and restriction are often incompletely specified. Defining disposition rules is upfront work not on the critical execution path. Specifying validation criteria for status transitions and release restrictions needs quality-systems expertise. Incomplete rule definition causes costly downstream disposition errors.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch execution entries whose responsible actor cannot be unambiguously attributed
rows.plain: Make sure the records for each production step clearly show who performed it. This creates a reliable record that can be explained to inspectors or reviewers.
rows.imp_rat: Entries whose responsible actor cannot be unambiguously attributed break ALCOA accountability; central to integrity and severe for inspection findings.
rows.sat_rat: Individual authentication makes responsible-actor attribution highly consistent, a well-served need except where shared or generic accounts persist. Unambiguous actor attribution is applied automatically in real time. Little reviewer skill needed once individual accounts are enforced. Leverages existing IAM infrastructure with negligible added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a job under a quality hold or release restriction is advanced in the active queue
rows.plain: A job that is being held back for quality checks or approval should not be moved forward in the active work list. This helps prevent work from continuing before it is cleared.
rows.imp_rat: Advancing a job under quality hold overlaps the hold-enforcement job of the MES (high relevance) and carries severe consequences of releasing restricted product; slightly redundant with item 11 but still core.
rows.sat_rat: Release-restriction enforcement is mature and reliable, with residual risk mainly at MES-to-scheduling handoffs. Queue restrictions apply immediately once a quality hold is set. Automated queue gating reduces dependence on schedulers manually tracking release status. Blocking held jobs from advancing avoids waste of downstream resources on restricted work.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of batch execution entries for which actor attribution cannot be established
rows.plain: Reduce the number of production records where it cannot be determined who performed each action. This helps show who did the work and supports trustworthy records.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to establish actor attribution undermines data-integrity structure (ALCOA); severe for accountability and inspection but data itself may still exist.
rows.sat_rat: Forced unique logins and automatic actor stamping make attribution highly reliable, a well-solved area barring shared credentials. Automatic user stamping on each entry is instantaneous. Attribution requires no special skill once logins are enforced. Unique-user attribution is inherent to the validated system with minimal marginal cost.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between a batch execution event and its corresponding record entry
rows.plain: Record each production run as soon as possible after it happens. This creates a clear record that can support claims that the rules were followed.
rows.imp_rat: Duration between event and record entry governs contemporaneousness, central to trustworthy capture; severe when latency lets memory-based errors enter.
rows.sat_rat: Contemporaneous EBR entry largely eliminates recording delay, though manual steps and offline operations can still introduce lag. Point-of-execution electronic entry sharply reduces the lag between event and record versus paper. Contemporaneous entry is prompted by workflow, needing little added operator skill. Real-time capture avoids costly after-the-fact reconstruction but requires shop-floor terminals.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a controlled production step proceeds with mandatory electronic batch record fields incomplete
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a required production step continues when important electronic records are unfinished. This helps ensure the needed information is recorded before work proceeds.
rows.imp_rat: Proceeding with incomplete mandatory eBR fields defeats the MES's record-integrity job (high relevance) and is severe because incomplete batch records can invalidate release and breach GMP.
rows.sat_rat: eBR mandatory-field enforcement is a mature, widely deployed control that reliably blocks incomplete records. Mandatory-field checks fire at record close without meaningful delay. Required-field enforcement removes reliance on operators remembering every mandatory entry. Complete-at-source records cut costly review-by-exception rework and batch-release delays.

rows.source_job: Connect shop-floor equipment to MES software
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of MES data points linked to incorrect PLC, sensor, or production-machine signals
rows.plain: Reduce the number of production readings connected to the wrong machine, sensor, or control signal. This helps ensure the production software shows accurate information.
rows.imp_rat: Data points linked to incorrect PLC/sensor/machine signals corrupt what the MES measures (relevant), and mislabeled signals can propagate errors into records (severity), though this is often a configuration-time issue caught in commissioning.
rows.sat_rat: Incorrect signal linkages are common because point-by-point verification is manual and rarely exhaustive. Mapping thousands of PLC/sensor signals to MES data points is a lengthy, tedious integration task. Correct signal-to-datapoint linkage needs controls engineers who understand both floor equipment and MES semantics. Integration labor is substantial though hardware cost is minimal.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which recorded timestamps fail to establish the required execution sequence
rows.plain: Record times clearly enough to show that each step happened in the required order. This helps reveal whether the procedure was followed correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Timestamps failing to establish the required execution sequence obscures order-dependent validity; relevant to structuring and severe when sequence is safety-critical.
rows.sat_rat: Contemporaneous system timestamps usually establish sequence well, though coarse granularity and manual entries can leave sequence undeterminable in places. Automatic timestamps eliminate manual time recording delays. System timestamps reduce reliance on operators to record execution times correctly. Timestamps that fail to establish sequence cause costly reconstruction effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical entries with ambiguous units, decimal placement, status, or exception qualifiers
rows.plain: Reduce the number of important records that are unclear about measurement units, decimal points, current status, or special exceptions. Clear records help prevent mistakes in carrying out the work.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous units, decimals, status, or exception qualifiers on critical entries invite misreading; relevant to structuring and severe when misread values change a pass/fail call.
rows.sat_rat: Structured data entry constrains many ambiguities, but free-text and exception qualifiers still leave meaningful interpretive risk. Structured units and formatted fields reduce time spent interpreting ambiguous entries. Enforced units and formats lessen reliance on reviewer interpretation of decimals and qualifiers. Ambiguous critical entries cause costly clarifications and potential misdisposition.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that relationships among timestamps, event order, record status, and disposition are misinterpreted
rows.plain: Make sure people correctly understand when records were made, what happened first, whether records are still valid, and what final decision was made. This helps prevent incorrect conclusions from the records.
rows.imp_rat: Misinterpreting relationships among timestamps, order, status, and disposition can lead to wrong conclusions; relevant to structuring and severe when it drives an incorrect disposition.
rows.sat_rat: Complex interrelationships among event order, status and disposition are frequently misread, and tooling offers limited interpretive support. Interpreting these relationships adds review time during disposition. Correctly reading timestamp, sequence, status and disposition interplay requires experienced reviewers. Misinterpretation causes costly rework and delayed releases.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of observed production deviations without a corresponding nonconformance record
rows.plain: Make sure every observed production problem is officially recorded, so issues can be investigated and corrected.
rows.imp_rat: Deviations without a corresponding nonconformance record is a compliance-critical gap (severity) affecting the control mandate, but it is a downstream quality-management concern relative to core execution (moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Only system-detected deviations reliably generate NCs; the many human-observed deviations frequently go unrecorded, making this poorly served. Automated NC creation from flagged deviations is fast, but detecting undocumented deviations still lags on manual observation. Capturing an observed deviation into an NC still depends heavily on staff recognizing and choosing to record it. Missing NC records drive costly audit findings and unmanaged quality risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable submission and inspection expectations omitted from gap criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the review rules cover all relevant expectations for applications and inspections. This helps prevent important compliance gaps from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting submission/inspection expectations from gap criteria is a criteria-design gap somewhat removed from core data capture; severe when it surfaces during inspection.
rows.sat_rat: Because expectations live in reviewers' heads rather than structured criteria, omissions from gap criteria recur unpredictably. Translating submission and inspection expectations into gap criteria is a lengthy manual exercise revisited each cycle. It hinges on experienced reviewers knowing what inspectors expect, and gaps appear where that tacit knowledge is uneven. Heavy reliance on senior expertise and post-hoc corrections drives ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a batch advancing past a required operation without completion
rows.plain: Prevent a batch from moving to the next step until the required operation is finished, so nothing is missed.
rows.imp_rat: A batch advancing past a required operation without completion breaks the MES's enforced sequencing (high relevance) and is severe since skipped steps yield non-conforming product.
rows.sat_rat: Sequential enforcement of operation completion is one of MES's most mature and dependable capabilities. Operation-completion gating checks are instantaneous and prevent time lost to rework from skipped steps. Enforced step sequencing removes the operator's burden of tracking completion manually. Preventing advance past incomplete operations avoids scrap and reprocessing costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of process parameter values recorded inaccurately in the MES
rows.plain: Record the machine's operating information accurately in the electronic production record, so people can trust the records and make sound decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurately recorded process parameters weaken the control job (high relevance) and can invalidate batch release decisions (meaningful severity), but effects are somewhat parameter-specific rather than systemic.
rows.sat_rat: Automated tags are accurate but the residual manual-entry portion keeps parameter recording only acceptably reliable. Automated parameter capture is fast, but manually entered parameters still consume operator time. Where parameters are hand-keyed, accuracy still depends on operator care, only partly reduced by validation prompts. Inaccurate parameters trigger investigations and rework that outweigh capture-system costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency between recorded event chronology and the documented execution sequence
rows.plain: Make sure the recorded order of events matches the order in which the work actually happened. This creates a reliable record of how the batch was made.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency between recorded and documented execution sequence undermines chronological truth; relevant to structuring and severe when sequence determines process validity.
rows.sat_rat: Timestamped events make sequence largely consistent, though manual steps and out-of-system events still introduce chronology mismatches. System event logs let reviewers compare recorded chronology to sequence quickly. Automated sequencing reduces the expertise needed to verify execution order. Chronology inconsistencies drive costly investigation and clarification.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required execution-record fields left blank after a batch step
rows.plain: Leave fewer required sections blank in the record after each production step. This reduces the need to correct or complete the paperwork later.
rows.imp_rat: Blank required fields after a step is a common capture gap; central to completeness but usually caught as reworkable rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Paper records routinely leave blanks that are caught only at review; electronic systems with mandatory fields do better where deployed. Filling every required field takes time and is often deferred, leaving temporary blanks. Knowing which fields are required relies on operator training rather than an enforced structure on paper. Blank fields force reviewer follow-up and late corrections that add administrative cost.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch events with inconsistent timestamps or execution sequences
rows.plain: Reduce the number of production records whose times or order of activities do not agree with each other. This helps show that the work happened in the recorded order and supports trustworthy records.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent timestamps or sequences undermine reconstructable chronology; relevant to structuring and severe when sequence determines process validity.
rows.sat_rat: Server-time stamping gives generally consistent sequencing, but integration across interfaced systems still produces occasional timestamp inconsistencies. System-generated timestamps are applied instantly at each event. Synchronized clocks reduce need for reviewer interpretation of sequence. Time synchronization is inexpensive standard infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the execution data required by a record field
rows.plain: Each record field should clearly state what information must be entered. This prevents workers from guessing and having to redo paperwork.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in what data a field requires causes inconsistent capture; relevant to structuring quality but consequences are usually correctable variability.
rows.sat_rat: Well-written master records reduce ambiguity but interpretation differences persist across operators. Ambiguous field requirements cause hesitation and clarification delays during entry. Resolving what a field wants depends on operator interpretation and training, exactly the skill burden this need targets. Ambiguity yields inconsistent entries that cost review effort to reconcile.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required batch execution entries absent when the record enters disposition review
rows.plain: Reduce the number of required production records that are missing when the batch is reviewed for approval. Complete records help show that the batch was made correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing required entries when the record enters disposition is detectable and reworkable, central to review readiness but lower severity when it triggers a hold rather than release.
rows.sat_rat: Structured EBR entry with mandatory fields makes absent required entries fairly rare at review entry, a well-served condition. Pre-review validation shortens time otherwise lost to returning incomplete records. Automated absence detection lessens the need for skilled manual completeness auditing. Reduced incomplete-record throughput lowers rework cost at review.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that production records subject to retention or hold obligations are irreversibly purged
rows.plain: Prevent production records that must be kept or protected from being permanently deleted. This ensures the records remain available when needed.
rows.imp_rat: Irreversible purging of records under retention/hold is infrequent (lower relevance to routine execution) but catastrophic when it happens, exposing the company to serious compliance and legal consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Regulated environments have retention controls, but purge routines occasionally reach records that should have been held, an irreversible failure. Retention holds are applied through policy engines that act promptly once triggered. Mapping which records fall under legal or regulatory hold demands compliance expertise that is not always available. Retention infrastructure exists but comprehensive coverage requires investment many sites limit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batches entering disposition review with required execution data missing
rows.plain: Send as few batches as possible to final review with required production information missing. Complete information helps the review finish faster.
rows.imp_rat: Batches entering review with missing data is largely detectable rework upstream; central to readiness but consequences are delay rather than unsafe release if caught.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic completeness verification catches most missing execution data before disposition, though edge cases from integrations still slip through. Completeness gating before disposition prevents delays from records bouncing back for missing data. System checks reduce the reviewer expertise needed to detect gaps before review begins. Fewer incomplete batches entering review cuts wasted review effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required execution data recorded in structures that prevent consistent disposition evaluation
rows.plain: Record required production information in a clear, consistent format that supports the final release decision. This helps reviewers reach and explain the right decision.
rows.imp_rat: Data recorded in structures preventing consistent disposition evaluation adds variability and review burden; relevant to usability but usually recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: EBRs enforce structure for core parameters but free-text and attachments still hold data in forms that resist consistent evaluation. Structured data speeds evaluation when present, but non-standard structures force manual interpretation. Interpreting inconsistently structured data requires expert judgement to normalize for evaluation. Reformatting or reconciling poorly structured data adds review labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a material batch-record discrepancy is discovered only during final disposition review
rows.plain: Find errors in a material’s production record before the final release review. This helps avoid delays at the end of the process.
rows.imp_rat: Discovering a discrepancy only at final disposition is costly rework and delay; moderately relevant since the error is eventually caught, limiting severity.
rows.sat_rat: Despite in-line checks, material discrepancies still surface at final review with troubling regularity because upstream review is not exhaustive. Late discovery at final review causes the worst schedule impact, and current in-process checks only partially prevent it. Catching material discrepancies early depends on skilled review at each stage, which is unevenly applied. Discrepancies found only at final disposition are the most expensive to resolve due to accumulated work.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of omitting critical dependencies between batch execution data and existing manufacturing and quality systems from the recommendation
rows.plain: Make sure the recommendation considers all important links between production records and the company’s existing production and quality tools. This helps the proposed approach work with what is already in place.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical dependencies on existing systems from a recommendation is an integration-planning gap peripheral to core data capture; severe mainly for downstream implementation risk.
rows.sat_rat: Without a systematic dependency-mapping practice, omissions are common and typically discovered only during validation or go-live, making outcomes inconsistent. Dependency discovery today relies on slow, iterative interviews across MES, LIMS, and QMS owners, so time pressure often forces the recommendation before all dependencies surface. Identifying hidden cross-system dependencies demands scarce integration architects who understand both GxP data flows and legacy interface quirks, and that expertise is rarely present on one team. Missed dependencies surface as expensive rework and change orders late in projects, but the status quo offers no cheap way to catch them up front.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of machine signals associated with incorrect MES tags
rows.plain: Make sure machine readings are matched to the correct production labels, so records about what happened are accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Machine signals mapped to incorrect MES tags is a tagging-integrity defect central to accurate data capture (moderate relevance) with moderate severity since it typically surfaces during validation before impacting live batches.
rows.sat_rat: Mis-tagged signals persist frequently because validation of each tag against live data is inconsistent. Tag mapping and verification across machine signals is slow when done thoroughly. Correct tag assignment requires engineers fluent in both machine addressing and MES tag conventions. The effort is labor-intensive but not materially costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of corrected batch entries with an incomplete correction rationale
rows.plain: When an error is corrected, clearly record why the correction was needed and what was changed. This makes the record understandable and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete correction rationale weakens defensibility of a change; relevant to integrity documentation but usually a moderate deficiency, not catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Rationale completeness is inconsistent because it depends on individual thoroughness. Writing a rationale takes modest time that operators sometimes shortcut. A complete rationale needs operator judgement about what to explain. Incomplete rationales cause reviewer follow-up costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of in-process batches with execution state lost or mismatched during replacement-system cutover
rows.plain: Reduce the number of production batches whose progress is lost or recorded incorrectly while operations are moved to replacement equipment. This helps prevent confusion and production errors during the changeover.
rows.imp_rat: Lost/mismatched batch state during replacement-system cutover is a one-time migration event (low routine relevance) but high severity, as in-process sterile batches could be scrapped or invalidated.
rows.sat_rat: Cutover of live in-process batches is one of the least reliable steps, with data model differences and timing gaps routinely causing lost or mismatched state despite best-effort reconciliation. Reconciling in-process batch execution state during cutover is time-consuming manual work that often extends the migration window. Correctly mapping and transferring live batch state between old and new systems demands deep expertise in both schemas, a rare and error-prone competency. State mismatches force batch holds, re-verification, or scrap, all of which consume significant labor and material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a completed MES backup is unusable for recovery because its content is corrupted
rows.plain: A finished copy of the production data should remain accurate and usable if it is needed to restore the system. Damaged or altered data could make recovery fail.
rows.imp_rat: An unusable corrupted backup is only relevant during disaster recovery (limited relevance to routine job) but severe when needed, potentially causing permanent loss of regulated production data.
rows.sat_rat: Backups complete routinely but their recoverability is frequently unverified, so unusable backups are a well-known lurking risk. Backups run on schedule automatically, so time is not the primary constraint. Judging backup usability requires periodic restore drills that skilled staff seldom perform routinely. Full restore-validation is costly, so many organizations skip it and discover corruption only during a real recovery.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of inspection and test results linked to the wrong batch, sample, or production step
rows.plain: Make sure inspection and test results are attached to the correct production batch, sample, and step. This keeps quality records accurate and traceable.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection/test results linked to the wrong batch or step corrupts quality decisioning (relevant) and could allow defective sterile product to be released (high severity), though the error is localized to quality-result linkage.
rows.sat_rat: Automated result linkage works well within MES but LIMS/instrument handoffs remain a recurring source of mislinked results. Context-driven linking of results to the active batch/sample is immediate at point of capture. System association reduces the technician's burden of manually mapping results to the right record. Correct linkage avoids costly mis-attribution investigations and re-testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production routings containing omitted required steps, materials, or resource assignments
rows.plain: Make sure each production plan includes every required step, material, and piece of equipment or staff support. Missing information can cause production to be incomplete or incorrect.
rows.imp_rat: Routings with omitted required steps or resource assignments affects execution correctness (relevant), with moderate severity since such omissions are often caught at routing validation before release.
rows.sat_rat: Routing templates and review checklists help, but omitted steps or resource assignments still slip through routinely. Building complete routings with all steps and resources is time-consuming during master-data setup. Routing authoring needs process engineers who know the full production sequence and BOM. Routing configuration is labor-heavy but a standard planned expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of consumption records containing an incorrect lot identity for a consumed material or component
rows.plain: Make sure each record correctly identifies the batch of material or part that was used. This makes it possible to trace what went into the product.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect lot identity in consumption records breaks material traceability (relevant to control) and in pharma can compromise recall scope and genealogy (notable severity), but it affects specific records rather than the whole execution engine.
rows.sat_rat: Scan-verified consumption is well established but manual overrides and unlabeled materials still let wrong-lot records through. Barcode/scan-based lot consumption is fast and avoids delays from manual lot lookup. Scan verification reduces reliance on operators reading and keying lot IDs correctly. Correct lot capture avoids costly traceability failures and recall scope expansion.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of execution values entered with an incorrect unit or format
rows.plain: Reduce the number of recorded results entered with the wrong measurement label or in the wrong required form. This saves time spent correcting records.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect unit or format entries introduce interpretation errors; moderately central and severe only when a unit error changes a pass/fail judgment.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-labeled units on forms and format masks in EBR make this fairly well controlled. Applying correct units/format is quick when the field is pre-formatted. Correct units depend on operator knowledge unless the field constrains input. Unit/format errors cause rework and potential misinterpretation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required execution fields left blank in the assembled batch record
rows.plain: Fill in every required part of the completed production record. This provides a full account of how the batch was made.
rows.imp_rat: Blank required fields in the assembled record is a completeness defect; central to the capture job but typically detected as rework before release.
rows.sat_rat: Required-field controls are a mature EBR strength, making blank execution fields relatively uncommon where properly configured. Real-time blank-field flagging prevents review-time back-and-forth over missing entries. Mandatory-field enforcement offloads completeness vigilance from reviewers to the system. Fewer blank fields reduce costly return cycles during review.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of units or lots whose genealogy records omit consumed materials, components, or production steps
rows.plain: Reduce the number of products or batches whose history leaves out materials used, parts added, or steps completed during production. This matters because people may be unable to trace how a product was made.
rows.imp_rat: Genealogy records omitting consumed materials or steps degrades traceability needed for recalls and audits (severity) and is fairly central to the measure/control job (relevance), yet remains a record-completeness issue rather than live control failure.
rows.sat_rat: Genealogy capture is a mature MES strength but omissions still occur at manual steps and unintegrated material sources. Genealogy is assembled automatically as material is consumed, saving time versus manual back-build. Automated as-built genealogy removes reliance on staff manually reconstructing the record. Complete genealogy avoids expensive recall-scope and traceability investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the recommended integration direction disrupts validated manufacturing or quality system functions
rows.plain: Make sure the recommended way of connecting systems does not interfere with approved manufacturing or quality operations. This helps avoid disruptions to production and oversight.
rows.imp_rat: A recommended integration direction disrupting validated manufacturing/quality functions is a system-design concern peripheral to record capture; severe if it destabilizes a validated system.
rows.sat_rat: Established change-control and impact-assessment practices catch many disruptions, yet subtle integration-induced breakages still slip through, keeping outcomes uneven. Impact assessments on validated functions are performed but take considerable time to trace through validated system boundaries. Judging disruption to validated manufacturing/quality functions requires deep validation and process knowledge that only senior SMEs reliably possess. Disruption to validated functions triggers costly revalidation, so avoiding it is a recognized priority but mitigated only through expensive conservative design.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of evaluation criteria based on inaccurate assumptions about manufacturing and quality system interfaces, dependencies, or data ownership
rows.plain: Use fewer judging rules based on incorrect ideas about how manufacturing and quality systems connect, depend on one another, or own information. This helps ensure decisions are based on how the work actually operates.
rows.imp_rat: Evaluation criteria based on inaccurate interface/ownership assumptions is a design-layer error somewhat removed from raw capture; severe when it misroutes data responsibility.
rows.sat_rat: Assumptions about data ownership and interfaces are frequently unverified and turn out wrong, so this is a chronic, poorly served failure point. Verifying assumptions about interfaces, dependencies, and data ownership requires time-consuming confirmation that projects often shortcut. Distinguishing accurate from inaccurate interface assumptions demands rare, specific knowledge of each system's actual data model and ownership boundaries. Criteria built on wrong assumptions drive rework and wasted procurement spend, with no inexpensive validation mechanism today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required execution-record field is unavailable when its batch step is performed
rows.plain: Make sure every required record field is available when the related production step is performed. This prevents workers from having to redo paperwork later.
rows.imp_rat: A required field unavailable when a step is performed impedes contemporaneous capture; relevant to workflow but usually a fixable availability issue.
rows.sat_rat: Master-record design usually anticipates fields well, so this surfaces relatively rarely. When a needed field is missing from the record, operators lose time improvising annotations. Handling a missing field requires operator judgement to record it elsewhere correctly. Missing fields cause deviations and record redesign costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of restored MES records that are missing or corrupted
rows.plain: Make sure as many restored production records as possible are complete and unchanged. This helps people rely on the records after a recovery.
rows.imp_rat: Restored MES records that are missing or corrupted matters mainly in recovery scenarios (lower everyday relevance) but carries high severity when it occurs, since lost production data can breach retention obligations.
rows.sat_rat: Standard backup/restore tooling recovers most records intact, but partial corruption or missing records surface often enough to remain a real gap. Restore operations themselves are reasonably quick, but verifying record completeness afterward adds time. Confirming restored records are intact needs staff familiar with data structures and reconciliation. Restore testing consumes resources and downtime that many sites ration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of WIP records showing incorrect material identity, quantity, location, or production status
rows.plain: Keep work records accurate about which materials are being used, how much there is, where it is, and what stage of production it has reached. Accurate records help workers find and manage unfinished products.
rows.imp_rat: WIP records showing incorrect material identity, quantity, or status hurts real-time production visibility (relevant), with moderate severity since operators can often reconcile discrepancies before serious loss.
rows.sat_rat: WIP accuracy is generally good but unrecorded moves, splits, and manual adjustments leave recurring discrepancies. Transaction-driven WIP updates reflect status in near real time. System-tracked WIP reduces dependence on operators manually maintaining status and location. Accurate WIP avoids costly search, over-production, and inventory write-offs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of operator actions and step completions left unrecorded at MES workstations
rows.plain: Make sure workers’ actions and completed steps are recorded at the workstations. Complete records show what was done and when.
rows.imp_rat: Unrecorded operator actions and step completions creates gaps in the execution record (relevant), with moderate severity because missing entries can trigger review holds but rarely cause irreversible product harm.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked step-by-step enforcement in modern MES makes skipping most steps difficult, but manual bypasses, offline moments, and non-enforced actions still leave a meaningful fraction unrecorded. Electronic work instructions and guided screens capture step completions in real time, so unrecorded actions rarely stem from slowness. Enforced electronic signoffs reduce the judgement operators need to record correctly, but recording still relies on operator diligence to actually click through each step. Gaps in the execution record cost rework and audit effort, and the workstation logging itself adds little marginal cost once deployed.

rows.source_job: Synchronize production data with connected systems
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of confirmed completions, consumptions, and quality results not received by connected enterprise systems
rows.plain: Make sure connected company computer systems receive records of completed work, used materials, and quality results. This keeps information consistent across the business.
rows.imp_rat: Confirmed completions/consumptions not received by enterprise systems breaks integration integrity (relevant to end-to-end control) with moderate severity since discrepancies are usually detectable and reconcilable.
rows.sat_rat: Mature MES-to-ERP integration with acknowledgement handling and retry queues catches the large majority of confirmations, yet edge-case failures and silent drops still leak into enterprise systems, leaving real recurring gaps. Standard messaging middleware and store-and-forward queues typically deliver confirmations to ERP within seconds, so timing is rarely the felt loss when this need fails. Once integration mappings are configured by specialists, day-to-day operators need no skill for transactions to flow, though troubleshooting a stuck interface still demands scarce IT/integration expertise. Missed transactions cause costly inventory and financial reconciliation work, but standard error-queue reprocessing keeps most of that cost contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of fill-finish pauses caused by required data capture
rows.plain: Shorten pauses in the filling process that are needed to record information. This helps the production run continue while still keeping the required records.
rows.imp_rat: Fill-finish pauses caused by data capture are largely an efficiency concern peripheral to capturing/structuring data; severity rises only where pauses stress aseptic conditions.
rows.sat_rat: Pauses are predictable but their duration varies with workflow design. Data capture routinely forces brief pauses in filling, directly extending run duration. Minimizing the pause depends on operator efficiency at the terminal. Pauses reduce throughput and raise per-batch cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of data-capture interruptions caused by operator-session expiration or disconnection
rows.plain: Reduce interruptions to recording information when a worker’s computer session ends or is disconnected. This helps the work continue without losing records or slowing production.
rows.imp_rat: Session expiration/disconnection interrupting capture is primarily a reliability/usability nuisance; relevant to timely capture but generally recoverable with low direct severity.
rows.sat_rat: In hybrid electronic setups session expiration is a recurring nuisance that interrupts capture. Session timeouts force re-login, adding disruptive delay to capture. Recovering from disconnection needs basic system familiarity. Lost sessions cause re-entry effort and occasional data loss cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that automatic failover does not activate when the active MES instance becomes unavailable
rows.plain: Make sure the backup computer system takes over automatically if the main production system stops working. This helps prevent operations from stopping unexpectedly.
rows.imp_rat: Failover not activating on instance failure is an availability-continuity concern (peripheral to the measure/control job itself) but severe when it strikes, causing production-line downtime for critical activities.
rows.sat_rat: Mature HA/failover technology works well when tested, though untested failover paths occasionally fail to activate. Configuring and testing failover clustering is a defined but non-trivial preparation activity. High-availability setup requires infrastructure specialists familiar with clustering and load balancing. Redundant nodes and HA licensing add meaningful cost that is usually budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of captured run, idle, or fault states differing from the actual machine state
rows.plain: The recorded machine status should match what the machine is really doing, whether it is running, waiting, or having a problem. This helps people and control systems respond to the true situation.
rows.imp_rat: Captured run/idle/fault states differing from actual machine state affects performance monitoring accuracy (moderate relevance) and moderate severity, as state mislabeling mainly distorts OEE rather than product quality directly.
rows.sat_rat: Integrated state capture is reliable on connected assets, but ambiguous micro-stops and unmapped fault codes still cause state mismatches. Machine-state capture via direct integration is real time with no reporting lag. Automated state detection removes reliance on operators manually classifying run/idle/fault. Accurate state capture underpins OEE-driven cost decisions and interface cost is modest once built.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the underlying cause of an MES fault
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the basic cause of a computer system problem will be mistaken. Finding the real cause helps prevent the wrong fix and further problems.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the underlying cause of an MES fault is fairly central to controlling production since it drives corrective action, and severity is moderate as wrong diagnosis prolongs downtime, but the ceiling caps this at 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Logs and diagnostics exist but are fragmented, so misattribution of fault causes remains common. Root-cause analysis of MES faults can drag on for days while production waits. Correctly diagnosing the underlying cause depends heavily on scarce senior engineers' experience and intuition. Prolonged investigations and misdiagnoses waste labor and downtime resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production records corrupted, duplicated, or lost after interface data flow resumes
rows.plain: Prevent production information from being damaged, repeated, or lost when a repaired system connection starts moving data again. This keeps the production record complete and accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Records corrupted/duplicated/lost after interface resumes is an edge-case recovery condition (low relevance) with moderate-to-high severity depending on reconciliation ability.
rows.sat_rat: Buffering helps, but data-flow interruptions routinely produce duplicated, lost or corrupted records that require painful cleanup. Reconciliation after interface resumption is time-consuming and often manual. Detecting duplicated or lost records across interfaces requires specialized integration knowledge. Message queuing and reconciliation tooling add cost that is unevenly deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of active batch-record unavailability at the point of operation
rows.plain: Keep the time that the current batch records are unavailable at the work area as short as possible. This helps workers record information when the work happens and avoid delays.
rows.imp_rat: Active record unavailability at the point of operation impedes contemporaneous capture; relevant to timely recording but typically a recoverable availability issue.
rows.sat_rat: Paper is nearly always available but electronic systems have downtime, so reliability is mixed. Active record unavailability at the point of operation stops recording entirely. Working around unavailability needs operator improvisation. Unavailability idles the line and risks lost data cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical deviations, quality holds, or interventions omitted from shift handover
rows.plain: Make sure the outgoing shift tells the incoming shift about all important problems, pauses, and special actions. This helps the next shift continue the work safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical deviations or quality holds from shift handover can propagate defects across shifts (moderate severity), but it is a handover-process error somewhat peripheral to the MES's core measure-and-control job.
rows.sat_rat: Shift handover is still largely verbal or free-text logbook practice weakly integrated with MES deviations, so critical holds and interventions are routinely lost across the shift boundary. Handover happens under end-of-shift time pressure, so critical items are often rushed rather than delayed per se. Effective handover depends heavily on an experienced outgoing operator knowing what matters and communicating it, making outcomes highly skill- and vigilance-dependent. Omitted deviations lead to expensive downstream quality escapes and duplicated interventions, and MES rarely structures handover to prevent this.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of point-of-operation entries requiring correction before execution can continue
rows.plain: Require corrections less often at the work station before the task can continue. This helps the production process keep moving without avoidable interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Point-of-operation entries requiring correction before continuing is mostly workflow friction; peripheral to record trustworthiness and low severity since errors are caught immediately.
rows.sat_rat: Validation prompts that block progress are inconsistently implemented, so this recurs. Stopping to correct point-of-operation entries before continuing halts execution. Getting entries right the first time depends on operator care. Correction-before-continue stalls the line, costing throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of MES transactions failing to reach the intended enterprise system
rows.imp_rat: MES transactions failing to reach enterprise systems directly undermines the integration that supports production control (fairly central), with moderate severity since data gaps can often be reconciled.
rows.sat_rat: Guaranteed-delivery messaging and retry queues are mature, but misconfigured transactions still occasionally fail to reach targets. Building and testing enterprise integration interfaces takes considerable preparation time. Integration middleware and message-queue expertise is required and moderately available. Middleware licensing and integration effort carry real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of unauthorized MES configuration changes left uncorrected
rows.imp_rat: Uncorrected unauthorized MES configuration changes can degrade control integrity and compliance (notable severity), but this is a governance error of moderate centrality to the core production-control job.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and audit trails catch many unauthorized changes, but some remain uncorrected between reviews. Detecting and reverting unauthorized changes can lag well behind when they occur. Distinguishing unauthorized from legitimate changes requires knowledgeable review of change logs. Continuous configuration monitoring adds tooling and staffing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a known MES vulnerability remains unpatched after a relevant security fix becomes available
rows.imp_rat: Leaving a known MES vulnerability unpatched exposes production systems to compromise (notable severity), yet security patching is a supporting maintenance activity of moderate centrality to the measure-and-control job.
rows.sat_rat: Validation caution keeps patches from breaking production, but leaves known vulnerabilities exposed far longer than in standard IT. Patch cycles for validated MES environments are slow because every change must be re-validated before deployment. Assessing patch relevance and validation impact requires specialized security and MES knowledge. Re-validation for each patch is costly, incentivizing deferral.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of progression blocks after completion of the required execution step
rows.plain: Have fewer times when work cannot move forward even though the required step is finished. This helps the production run continue without needless waiting.
rows.imp_rat: Progression blocks after a completed step are a usability/availability annoyance largely peripheral to data integrity; low severity as they cause delay, not bad data.
rows.sat_rat: System logic sometimes over-blocks, an inconsistent but recurring hindrance. Progression blocks after a completed step cause avoidable waiting. Clearing a block may need supervisor or system knowledge. Blocks idle the line and staff, costing throughput.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time spent waiting for a required source value or identifier at the recording point
rows.plain: Reduce the time workers spend waiting for a needed number, value, or name before they can record it. This keeps the work moving without unnecessary pauses.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for a source value/identifier at recording is an efficiency delay peripheral to the integrity of captured data; low severity, mainly slows throughput.
rows.sat_rat: Availability of source identifiers is inconsistent, causing intermittent waits. Waiting for a source value or identifier at the recording point is a direct delay. Knowing where to obtain the value quickly relies on operator experience. Waiting idles the operator and slows the batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of disposition conclusions lacking traceable support from cited batch evidence
rows.plain: Make sure every final decision about a production batch is supported by records that clearly show why the decision was made. This makes the decision easier to check and defend.
rows.imp_rat: Disposition conclusions lacking traceable evidence support strike at the core reviewability of captured data (relevance 4) with serious compliance consequence (severity 4), held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Disposition conclusions frequently lack explicit citation traceability because rationale is often narrative and manually assembled. Building traceable evidence support into conclusions is time-consuming under current documentation practice. Constructing traceable rationale linking evidence to conclusions demands skilled reviewers and is inconsistently done. Untraceable conclusions trigger audit findings and rework, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved deviations recorded as resolved before batch disposition
rows.plain: Fewer problems should be marked as settled before the final decision is made about a production batch. This helps ensure that unresolved issues are not overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations recorded as resolved before disposition is a falsification-adjacent error central to reliable review (relevance 4) with severe release consequence (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Deviation workflows help, but deviations marked resolved without true closure still slip through, undermining confidence. Detecting falsely-resolved deviations adds review time when caught. Distinguishing genuinely resolved from prematurely closed deviations requires seasoned QA judgement. Premature closures that surface later drive costly reopenings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of mandatory batch evidence missing when disposition is rendered
rows.plain: Make sure all required batch records are available before making the final decision about the batch. This supports a complete and informed decision.
rows.imp_rat: Mandatory batch evidence missing when disposition is rendered is a core completeness failure (relevance 4) with serious consequence for valid release (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory-field enforcement and completeness checks in EBRs make missing required evidence uncommon. Assembling mandatory evidence takes time but the failure is missing evidence at decision. Knowing which evidence is mandatory is codified in review checklists, limiting skill dependence. Missing evidence forces re-review cycles that waste QA resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical deviations or out-of-specification investigations unresolved at disposition
rows.plain: Fewer serious problems or investigations into results outside the allowed range should still be unresolved when a final decision is made. This helps ensure products are not approved before important issues are settled.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved critical deviations or OOS investigations at disposition is a core completeness failure (relevance 4) with severe release consequence (severity 4), capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory closure gates for critical/OOS items are standard, making unresolved-at-disposition rare in mature systems. Critical investigations are long-running and their pendency delays disposition, but the need is about them being unresolved. Adjudicating critical deviations and OOS results requires senior investigator expertise. Unresolved critical items force holds that tie up capital and can scrap batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the final disposition rationale is unsupported by approved evidence and applicable standards
rows.plain: Base the final decision about the batch on approved records and the rules that apply. The reasoning should be supported by clear evidence rather than guesswork.
rows.imp_rat: A final disposition rationale unsupported by approved evidence/standards defeats the purpose of structured review (relevance 4) with serious compliance impact (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Structured disposition templates enforce evidence citation, though completeness of support still varies. Documenting a supported rationale takes time but the failure is inadequate support, not lateness. Constructing a defensible rationale tied to evidence and standards requires strong regulatory writing skill. Unsupported rationales invite costly audit findings and re-review.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required disposition approvals that are missing or invalid
rows.plain: Reduce the number of required approvals for deciding what happens to a product that are missing or not valid. This helps ensure the decision is properly authorized.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or invalid required disposition approvals is a core gate failure (relevance 4) with serious release-legitimacy consequence (severity 4), held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory signature workflows reliably prevent missing or invalid approvals in validated EBRs. Collecting approvals adds routing time but the error is their absence or invalidity. Ensuring approval validity is enforced by system rules, not reviewer skill. Missing approvals trigger rework and audit exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that disposition proceeds before all relevant deviations are adjudicated
rows.plain: Do not make a final decision about the material or batch until every important problem has been reviewed and resolved. This helps prevent affected material from being accepted too soon.
rows.imp_rat: Disposition proceeding before all deviations are adjudicated is a core review-completeness failure (relevance 4) with serious release consequence (severity 4), held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: EBR/deviation-management integration usually blocks disposition until open deviations close, giving strong control. Waiting for all deviations to close before disposition is a recognized source of delay but the error is premature proceeding. Tracking that every relevant deviation is adjudicated is largely procedural rather than expertise-limited. Proceeding early risks costly rework and regulatory citations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch records advanced to disposition with release-relevant deviations unresolved
rows.plain: Fewer production batches should move to the final decision while important problems affecting their release are still unresolved. This helps prevent unsuitable batches from being approved.
rows.imp_rat: Advancing records to disposition with release-relevant deviations unresolved is a central review-gate failure (relevance 4) with serious safety/compliance stakes (severity 4), at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Linked deviation status flags in EBRs reliably surface open items, though relevance filtering is imperfect. Chasing unresolved deviations before disposition consumes time but the target is the unresolved state itself. Identifying release-relevant deviations still needs qualified reviewer judgement. Advancing records with open deviations wastes downstream QA effort and delays inventory turns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch records lacking valid required approval before disposition
rows.plain: Fewer production batches should reach the final decision without all required, valid approvals. This helps ensure that only properly reviewed batches are approved.
rows.imp_rat: Batch records lacking valid required approval before disposition is a core gate failure (relevance 4) with serious compliance consequence (severity 4), held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic workflow enforces required e-signatures before disposition, making invalid approvals rare. Approval routing takes time but the failure is a missing or invalid approval. Verifying valid approval is procedural, requiring little specialized skill. A missing approval invalidates release and forces costly rework.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch-data handoffs lacking validation criteria for content, sequence, and attribution
rows.plain: Make sure every transfer of production records has checks for what information is included, its order, and who entered it. This helps keep records complete and tied to the right person or step.
rows.imp_rat: Batch-data handoffs lacking content/sequence/attribution validation directly threaten faithful capture and structuring of execution data (relevance 4); severity is meaningful since broken handoffs corrupt the review record.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-data handoffs between systems often lack rigorous validation criteria, leaving attribution and sequence gaps. Defining handoff criteria adds analysis time across system boundaries. Specifying content, sequence and attribution at handoffs needs cross-system integration expertise. Weak handoff validation leads to costly data-integrity failures at interfaces.

rows.stk: Data Integrity / Computer System Validation Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of electronic signature controls omitted from validation criteria for batch review and disposition
rows.plain: Fewer rules for confirming who approved an electronic record should be left out when checking systems used to review and decide on products. This helps make approvals trustworthy and traceable.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting e-signature controls from validation criteria weakens attribution central to capturing reviewable batch data (relevance 4), and severity is moderate since missing signature validation can invalidate disposition, but ranking caps this below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: E-signature controls are commonly validated but coverage of every review/disposition point in criteria is sometimes incomplete. E-signature validation is defined once during system validation. Part requirements are well documented, reducing reliance on rare expertise. Signature controls are standard EBR features with modest validation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which evaluation criteria permit loss of batch data lineage, source attribution, chronology, or context across system boundaries
rows.plain: The evaluation rules should prevent the loss of information showing where each batch came from, what happened to it, and when and why it happened as data move between systems. This makes the records easier to trust and investigate.
rows.imp_rat: Criteria permitting loss of data lineage/attribution/chronology across boundaries undermines the very integrity of captured batch data (relevance 4, severity 4), held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practices catch gross lineage breaks, but subtle loss of source attribution and context across boundaries remains a common, under-caught failure. Tracing lineage and context loss across boundaries is a slow analytical task that competes with delivery timelines. Detecting subtle context or lineage loss across system handoffs requires specialized data-governance skill that is not universally available. Lineage loss surfaces as costly investigations and data reconciliation later, with little upfront preventive investment today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch dispositions with broken evidentiary chains across actions, exceptions, assessments, and approvals
rows.plain: Fewer final decisions about production batches should have gaps in the connected records showing actions, exceptions, reviews, and approvals. This helps others trace how and why each decision was made.
rows.imp_rat: Broken evidentiary chains across actions/exceptions/approvals directly break the structured record needed for review (relevance 4) with serious traceability consequence (severity 4), capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: EBR systems link some records automatically, but evidentiary chains still break across systems and exception handling. Reconstructing evidentiary chains across actions, exceptions, and approvals is time-consuming when links are not native. Tracing chains requires experienced reviewers to mentally connect disparate records. Broken chains cause costly re-investigation and inspection findings.

rows.source_job: Import master data into MES software
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of imported source values assigned to incorrect MES fields or codes
rows.imp_rat: Assigning imported source values to incorrect MES fields corrupts data used for control, giving moderate relevance, but such mapping errors are usually detectable and correctable, bounding severity.
rows.sat_rat: Import mapping errors are common because full reconciliation of every field and code is rarely completed. Field-by-field data mapping and code translation is a slow, detailed preparation task. Correct mapping needs analysts who understand both source and MES data models. Mapping and reconciliation labor is significant but not material-heavy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required interventions, stoppages, rejects, and batch events omitted from production reports
rows.imp_rat: Omitting interventions, stoppages, and batch events from production reports weakens visibility into production, a moderately central concern, though the underlying control still functions and consequences are moderate.
rows.sat_rat: MES reports faithfully reflect whatever was captured, so their completeness is only as good as event capture — interventions and rejects logged out-of-band still get omitted at a moderate rate. Automated report generation pulls logged events on demand, so production reports are produced quickly once data exists. Reports auto-compile without analyst effort, but completeness depends on operators having correctly recorded events upstream. Missing events distort yield and OEE accounting and drive misinformed decisions, though the reporting engine itself is cheap to run.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that corruption in stored MES backups remains undetected after integrity verification
rows.imp_rat: Undetected corruption in MES backups is severe when a restore is needed since data may be unrecoverable, but backup integrity is a peripheral recovery concern rather than central to controlling live production.
rows.sat_rat: Basic checksums catch gross corruption, yet subtle undetected corruption in stored backups remains a recognized blind spot. Integrity checks run in the background without much added wait. Interpreting integrity-check results and configuring meaningful verification requires backup-domain expertise. Checksum verification is cheap to run but thorough deep verification is often skipped to save resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production-impacting interface deviations remaining undetected after integration validation
rows.imp_rat: Undetected interface deviations after integration validation can silently disrupt production data flow, but this is a validation-completeness error of moderate centrality and consequence to the core control job.
rows.sat_rat: Validation catches many deviations, but production-impacting ones still escape into live operation with notable frequency. Integration validation cycles are lengthy and often compressed under schedule pressure. Designing test cases that expose production-impacting interface deviations needs deep integration knowledge. Building realistic integration test coverage is expensive, so gaps persist.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of classifying an incompatible ERP, SCADA, or control-system interface as compatible
rows.imp_rat: Classifying an incompatible ERP/SCADA interface as compatible risks integration failure, but it is a validation-judgment error caught before go-live, giving moderate relevance and recoverable severity.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility is often asserted rather than tested at acquisition, so incompatible interfaces are frequently misclassified as compatible until deployment. Interface compatibility checks stretch acquisition timelines because they depend on vendor responses and integration specialists' availability. Judging true ERP/SCADA/control-system compatibility from datasheets requires rare integration expertise that is easily overestimated during selection. Paper compatibility claims cost little to accept but drive large integration overruns when wrong.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an actionable deviation indication is overlooked during concurrent run demands
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking an actionable deviation indication under concurrent-run load is a serious miss (severity 4) but concerns operational vigilance more than the capture/structuring job (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Human vigilance under load is unreliable, so this is poorly served without automated alerting. Noticing a deviation indication amid concurrent demands can be delayed. Not overlooking an actionable indication under multitasking is a severe attention/vigilance burden this need targets. Overlooked deviations lead to costly batch impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect approved range is applied to a critical process parameter during the run
rows.imp_rat: Applying an incorrect approved range to a critical parameter during the run risks wrong pass/fail (severity 4); relevance moderate as an in-run control adjacent to review capture (relevance 3).
rows.sat_rat: Pre-specified ranges in the master record make wrong-range application uncommon. Confirming the correct approved range is applied is a quick reference check. Applying the right range depends on operator/config knowledge of the batch spec. A wrong range can pass an out-of-spec batch, an extreme cost.
